abstract:
This volume examines the critical paradox of contemporary education: how systemic efforts toward inclusion frequently generate new and more refined forms of exclusion. By drawing on a diverse range of international perspectives—including case studies from Australia, Europe, Africa, and Asia—the contributors analyze the "exclusionary pressures" that undermine equitable schooling. The book interrogates the role of neoliberal management, the labeling of special educational needs, and the impact of rigid behavioral policies on marginalized groups.

Across three thematic sections, the collection explores how institutional structures and policy frameworks disadvantage specific populations, including Indigenous communities, refugees, Roma children, and those affected by gender-based violence. By revisiting the theoretical foundations of inclusive education and scrutinizing the practical realities of segregation and suspicion within school systems, the authors highlight the "wicked problems" that prevent genuine belonging. Ultimately, this work offers a rigorous critique of the global education landscape, identifying the mechanisms through which inclusionary rhetoric can mask exclusionary practices.
